content: Woolly mammoths lived during the Pleistocene epoch, from about 5 million years ago until roughly 4,000 years ago. They inhabited the mammoth steppe — a vast, cold grassland stretching across northern Europe, Asia, and North America. Temperatures were much colder than today, with massive ice sheets covering much of the northern hemisphere.

Woolly mammoths had thick fur up to 90 centimetres long, a layer of fat 8 centimetres thick, and small ears and tails to minimize heat loss. They stood about 3.4 metres tall at the shoulder — similar to modern African elephants. Their iconic curved tusks could reach 4.2 metres long and were used for sweeping snow to reach grass beneath.

Mammoth extinction was driven by two forces: climate change and human hunting. As the last Ice Age ended around 12,000 years ago, warming temperatures shrank the grasslands mammoths depended on. Simultaneously, skilled human hunters with stone-tipped spears hunted them. Mammoths reproduced slowly — one calf every 4-5 years — making population recovery impossible.

While most woolly mammoths disappeared by 10,000 years ago, a small population survived on Wrangel Island in the Arctic Ocean until about 4,000 years ago — meaning they were alive when the Egyptian pyramids were being built. This isolated population became dwarfed over time and eventually died out, possibly due to inbreeding and loss of genetic diversity.

Because many mammoths were preserved in permafrost, their DNA has been remarkably well-preserved. In 2023, the biotechnology company Colossal Biosciences announced plans to recreate mammoth-like elephants using woolly mammoth DNA combined with Asian elephant genetics. These 'mammophants' would be cold-adapted elephants designed to help restore Arctic grasslands.

Dozens of remarkably preserved mammoth remains have been found in Siberian permafrost, some with intact skin, fur, and even stomach contents. In 2013, scientists discovered a mammoth so well-preserved that liquid blood was found inside. The baby mammoth 'Lyuba', found in 2007, is the best-preserved mammoth specimen ever discovered, dating to 42,000 years ago.

Early humans hunted mammoths for food, used their bones and tusks to build shelters, and carved ivory tools and artwork. Cave paintings in France and Spain depict mammoths alongside other Ice Age animals. The relationship between humans and mammoths shaped early human technology, art, and migration patterns across continents.

The mammoth extinction serves as a powerful lesson about climate change. When habitats change faster than species can adapt, extinction follows. Today's climate change is happening much faster than the post-Ice-Age warming, threatening thousands of species. Understanding mammoth extinction helps us predict and prevent modern biodiversity loss.
